---
title: "About"
---
*WEBSITE STILL WIP*


Hi there! I’m Edward, an undergrad in the class of 2024 and an Egleston Scholar at Columbia Engineering.

As of now, my main interests lie amidst the fields of Machine Learning and Computational Neuroscience. My lifetime goal is to contribute to the ongoing pursuit of comprehending what exactly governs consciousness and sentience, which will *hopefully* happen some day (and also *hopefully* not create something along the lines of Ultron or Skynet).

Currently, most of my time is going into a research project with Dr. Nakul Verma at Columbia's Computer Science department and a subcontract software engineering job at DATAFLUCT, Inc.

I'm currently working on research on Representation Learning—specifically, regarding the mathematical properties of the Skip-Gram model. While there's already been a significant amount of research done on the underlying properties of the Skip-Gram model, it suffices to say that we still don't understood it too well and we don't have good explanations as to why the model has the surprising qualities it has (additive composition, vectors occupy a narrow cone, etc.). In a nutshell, I want to see how certain changes in the Skip-Gram model affect downstream tasks and what sort of modifications lead to changes in the properties of the Skip-Gram model.

At DATAFLUCT, Inc, I'm currently working on developing a dialogue system that provides BI (Business Intelligence) and data analytics results as the lead (or rather, lone) software engineer for the backend (and a bit of frontend) side. Apparently I can't disclose much, but almost no ML is involved so far and most of the logic is purely heuristic. Nevertheless, it's an eye-opening experience working with (way) more experienced developers who are working on different parts of the system.

Besides doing research and working, I'm also in Columbia Space Initiative (CSI), Columbia's space-themed club. I was in the RASC-AL team that participates in an annual student competition to design deep space mission concepts. Specifically, I was in charge of designing the propulsion systems (more or less just reading past papers on the topic and coming up with new designs based on previously successful missions).

While I was in Columbia's Formula Racing Club (FSAE), I was working on tire simulation; specifically, applying curve-fitting and coefficient optimization on Pacejka's Magic Formula.

In my free time, I like to program—mainly simulations that I find interesting to see what would happen and reinforcement learning agents to train on the games I make. I also like making robots and tinkering (and Columbia has the perfect space for that!).

If you're interested, here are a few miscellaneous links (most are in Japanese) where I appear:

- [The Egleston Scholars: Weida (Edward) Li](https://www.engineering.columbia.edu/egleston-scholars/weida-edward-li)
- [江副記念財団学術部門第四十九期生：李 為達](https://www.recruit-foundation.org/academic/li/)
- [Containerインタビュー：李 為達](https://container-web.jp/people/15564.html)
- [現役高校生チーム、アメリカのロボット大会に臨む！](https://newswitch.jp/p/10882)

Feel free to reach out to me for anything!

- Email: [wl2787@columbia.edu](mailto:wl2787@columbia.edu)
- GitHub: [NarutatsuRi](https://github.com/narutatsuri)
